data.title: BYD's Vertical Moat: How Integration And Expansion Could Redefine EV Leadership
data.description:
Overview

BYD Company Limited (OTCPK:BYDDF, OTCPK:BYDDY) has of late become an actively discussed company in an active industry – EV. BYD has several other revenue segments as well, but the core business segment that contributes majorly is the automotive segment. The graph shows the stock returns of BYDDF in the past one year.

The stock has generated a staggering 31.83% returns which is impressive even by EV industry standards, because even the Global X Autonomous & Electric Vehicles ETF (DRIV) generated 21.38% returns in the same period.

One of the most important differentiating factors has been the level of vertical integration that BYD has achieved in the EV space, which fortifies it against any vulnerabilities. Apart from automotives, BYD also operates in new energy (energy storage systems, solar panels, etc) as well as IT & electronics. Note that the Chinese government recently announced a plan to nearly double battery storage by 2027. BYD’s diversity is not restricted only to revenue segments, but also extends to product portfolios within the automotive segment as well.

Until this time, BYD’s revenue had been mostly concentrated in China. However, recently they have been expanding in other countries as well, like setting up the first US battery plant to service the North American demand.

Having stated these, the EV industry is now beginning to face difficulties, like rising competition, gradual drying up of government support, etc. What remains to be seen is where BYD stands among these different pushes and pulls.

Tailwind

Complete Vertical Integration

One of the greatest strengths of BYD is the level of vertical integration that it has achieved. The beginning part of the value chain is the mining of the rare earth metals. The Chinese businesses and the Chinese government has realised early on that power in the 21st century lies in having access to these very rare earth metals as these are used in all products ranging from daily electronic items to defense equipment. Today China controls the supply of 70% of the rare earth metals and 90% of the processing.

BYD has taken steps to set up long-term partnerships with mining companies and also bought stakes in different mining companies as well. For example, BYD has taken up mining rights in Brazil’s Lithium Valley. This protects China from external shocks, especially at a time when the world is witnessing significant geopolitical volatility.

Narrowing down to electric vehicles, there are big four rare earth metals that are used are Neodymium, Praseodymium, Dysprosium and Terbium. The graphs below show the price chart of some of the metals.

Both the charts follow very similar patterns. The prices reached their peak around the middle of 2021 and then faced a decrease post that. However, the prices have again begun decreasing again.

However, the benefits of vertical integration are not just restricted to ownership of mines. BYD also has ownership of the other part in between as well.

Post the extraction of the raw materials, BYD also has the infrastructure built in to refine to bring it up to battery-grade chemicals. BYD has invested heavily to improve its refining capacity through its subsidiary FinDreams. FinDreams operates as a global battery manufacturer and energy storage company. It not only helps in the vertical integration, but also helps in diversifying the revenue sources of BYD as well. Moreover, this is not where it stops. FinDreams has also laid out its industry chain which is given below.

What I believe that makes it stand out is its circular nature. It only goes on to highlight that FinDreams is already positioning itself for recycling of the mineral resources which makes it relatively less vulnerable to prices and supply of new raw materials.

One of the biggest contributions by FinDreams to the EV value chain is the creation of the blade battery technology.

The blade battery uses lithium iron-phosphate as the cathode material which provides a higher level of safety vis-a-vis conventional lithium-ion batteries. Given below is an excerpt from the official BYD website on the safety test of the battery.

During the Nail Penetration Test, the Blade Battery gave off no smoke or fire and the surface temperature only reached 30 to 60 degrees Celsius. It also withstood other extreme test conditions, such as being crushed, bent, heated in an oven to 300 degrees Celsius and overloaded by 260%. None of these resulted in a fire or explosion, making BYD Blade Battery a safety leader for the burgeoning EV market.

BYD Official Website

The benefits offered by the battery apart from safety are manifold. These include, efficiency, extended range, greater power density and faster charging. It is worth noting that the benefits offered by the battery are ones that will appeal to the average EV buyer. Given below is an excerpt from what an average Chinese middle class individual had to say about BYD when compared with Tesla.

BYD is no longer just a cheap brand — their quality has improved a lot. The Blade battery is very safe, cars are comfortable and modern, and for daily use they’re perfect. Tesla still feels better if you want sporty driving and sharp acceleration, but for most families here BYD is more practical, cheaper, and easier to service.

An average Chinese customer

Often many car makers run into bottlenecks when it comes to sourcing of semiconductors. The problem was particularly acute in the period of 2020 to 2023. However, BYD was smart enough to be ahead of its time and create a hedge against this problem. BYD had set up BYD Semiconductors in 2004 which subsequently went on to become a key pillar for BYD.

Possible Foray into Indian Market Amid Global EV Expansion

The recent thawing of relations between India and China opens up the scope for BYD EVs making their foray into the Indian market which is having increased potential for the EV market. According to news reports, BYD senior management is set to visit India. This may have the potential to be a breakthrough for BYD as the new centers for EV growth are Asia and South America. BYD already has a significant presence in China. Access to the Indian market may unlock significant value post this.

This is taking place amid the global context when the EV market is still growing rapidly. Inspite of the economic uncertainty, by 2030, EV sales as a percentage of total car sales will exceed 40% based on current policy settings. China stands to benefit from this massive share of EV sales. Note that global EV sales grew by 28% in one year and in China it grew by 33%. Given below are the growth figures of EV sales across different regions of the world.

  • Global: 7.2 million, +28%

  • China: 4.4 million, +33%

  • Europe: 1.6 million, +27%

  • North America: 0.7 million, +3%

  • Rest of World: 0.6 million, +36%

What is worth noting is that only North America currently has a very low growth rate. This tells us another story. Today the primary and greatest competitor that BYD is facing is from Tesla in the EV space both because of their size as well as the government support they are receiving from the respective governments. Both of the EV makers have their primary market in their own respective countries. However, this is where the growth story plays in. With North America growth in lower single digit values, it leaves very little scope for Tesla’s domestic business expansion while for BYD it is still likely to witness significant domestic business growth.

Having said that, both the EV makers are trying to expand their businesses worldwide. Tesla’s expansion is made obvious by the relatively lower growth rate. However, BYD’s expansion is driven by their aim to provide just-in-time services which is also aided by the level of vertical integration that BYD has achieved.

Having discussed the positive aspects, it is also worth noting that today China is a significant EV market. But for the future, India is likely to be a significant market, given the already growing nature of the industry as well as the high growth economy. The government itself has also set targets of 30% EV adoption.

The problem that arises here is that the Indian government may put up entry barriers to build up the domestic EV industry as well as to encourage FDI. BYD may face high tariffs like the Tesla EVs have been facing. However, I believe that there is unlikely to be this problem because China holds the card here because they supply the raw materials, especially the rare-earth metals, for India’s domestic EV production as well. This will allow China to use this power in negotiation to possibly gain access to the Indian market without high entry barriers.

China benefitting in the EV market translates to BYD benefitting because it is the largest EV manufacturer in China as of today.

Headwinds

Phasing out of Government Support

One of the biggest headwinds for the EV industry is the gradual phasing out of the government support that is provided. The figure below provides the situation currently prevailing for the global market.

The share of spending from the government has steadily fallen since 2017. It may be argued that this has been due consumer spending outpacing the spending from the government, the figure also highlights that government spending has mostly stagnated since 2022. Now note that while consumer spending has increased, we are heading towards a global economic slowdown, and in China the problem may become especially acute with the tariff war, and also the housing crisis which may reduce the purchasing power of the consumers.

According to reports, there has been significant wiping out of household wealth due to the property crisis. This will directly impact consumer discretionary spending and purchase of electric vehicles is one such area of spending that will directly be impacted. Added to this is the demographic problem that China has been facing where the share of the young population has been steadily declining as well. All these factors combined pose a significant risk for the heavy exposure of BYD to the Chinese market and that is an important reason as to why BYD is looking for international diversification.

Rising Competition

One of the biggest threats to BYD and also the EV industry in particular is the rising level of competition. As the industry becomes increasingly popular, it threatens the existing as more players enter the space. The figure below shows the market share of the key players in China’s NEV market as of 2025.

It is worth noting that Tesla has gained a substantial share of 5.1% and is aiming to pick up the market share as the demand from USA dries up and an emerging economy like India still remains largely inaccessible. Moreover, there have been increasing entries of new players as well. Therefore, with the new entry and gradual withdrawal of government support, it indicates that there remains very little moat for BYD that makes it defensible in the EV business.

However, BYD is already ahead and the first mover advantage is likely to sustain for upcoming years as BYD keep upgrading its vehicles. Largely consumers prefer BYD vehicles over any others and therefore, rising competition does not remain a significant concern as yet.

Financials & Valuation

Before moving to the valuation of BYD and understanding in quantitative terms whether BYD is worth putting money in for long-term investors, it will be a good measure for understanding the previous trend in financials of BYD and how they have been performing. The figure below highlights the revenue level and the profit margins of BYD.

It is worth noting that the sales level has grown rapidly since 2021 the trajectory is expected to continue as well. Moreover, before 2022, the profit margins were fluctuating rapidly, stabilizing post 2022. Moreover, note that the gap between operating profit margin and net income margin has also narrowed. This is largely attributed to the decrease in net interest expense.

Therefore, the story this graph tells us is that BYD has gradually positioned itself as a major EV maker with stabilising profit which promises to be a stable source of returns for investors.

One of the challenges of valuing a company like BYD is the difficulty in forecasting the cash flow schedule for the future. BYD operates in an industry which is not only at a relatively nascent stage, but is also vulnerable to the numerous external movements that are currently hitting the industry like geopolitical tensions, tariffs, rare earth metal shortage, etc. The volatility is further highlighted when comparing the profit levels and the cash flows as given below.

Therefore, the approach that I adopted was to follow a relative valuation method based on the industry median as given below and getting to an estimate of the target price. The figure below shows the same. The picture clearly shows that on numerous metrics, BYD is currently undervalued. The lower value of the ratios would have been justified had BYD been an underperformer in the EV and new energy space. However, as I have already explored, BYD is among the top performers with several defensible features like the complete vertical integration as well as government support. It has several other beneficial factors like continued EV growth and possible foray into the Indian government. It only solidifies the argument as to why the ratio values should be closer to the industry median or even higher.

The reason that I believe that investors do not yet value BYD as an investment opportunity is because of the fear that they have of the Chinese market and the headwinds that will spill over into the Chinese EV market as a result which will directly impact the business prospects of BYD.

However, as I have already explained, it is rather unlikely that by the time we get to witness a bear run in the Chinese EV market, BYD will already have taken actions to diversify its revenue sources, like it is already doing.

Conclusion

Having analysed BYD business model and prospects thoroughly, I believe that for investors looking for long term value, BYD is going to prove a value investment as the company remains in a strongly defensible position. This is further aided by the first mover advantage, government support and reading the consumer preference. The prospects are further enhanced by the level of vertical integration that it has achieved. The valuation numbers highlight how investors are yet to realise the full potential of BYD as of yet and now might be the best time to put money in before the market realises BYD’s potential and the share prices rise.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: EWP: After The Sunshine Comes The Storm
data.description:
If you want to invest in Spain, the iShares MSCI Spain ETF (NYSEARCA:EWP) is the only U.S.-listed ETF exclusively focused on the country. The attention around this ETF has recently increased due to dazzling YTD performance close to +60%; for a developed country, this is unusual. Even in Europe, the SPDR® EURO STOXX 50® ETF (FEZ), representative of the region, is not growing this much.

In this analysis we look at EWP to determine if investing now makes sense. The ETF is very concentrated on its key holdings, and this high level of concentration is the source of the explosive growth. However, I think that now is a time to wait for a correction before buying, and I give EWP a hold rating.

Spain’s Market Surge: Price dynamics

YTD, EWP returned 58.75% growth. This is twice as much as FEZ, and its 28.51% is actually very unusual for Spanish markets. You will find below a long term price chart to see how odd this growth is. My concern is that the price movement of EWP is very cyclical, with clear “up” and “down” events. The recent price surge appears to be an “up” moment, and the next logical step is a “down” moment.

In addition, this fulgurant growth is not matching macroeconomic fundamentals. The Spanish GDP is projected to end 2025 with a 2.7% growth. This is better than expected earlier in the year (2.6%), but still not enough to justify such a stock rally. However, this is above the growth of the Eurozone, expected to be only 1.2%. Naturally seeing higher growth in Spain than in the region makes sense, but this is a big delta, and I believe that the recent price surge has captured the medium-term upside investors were expecting. I don’t see the price going much higher from this point.

If we look at more recent time frames, such as 1M (-0.18%) and 1W (0.58%), it is clear that the growth momentum is coming to an end, and that the opportunity has passed. What remains now is a high level of risk, far more than the potential additional upside you can expect.

I will consider EWP as a buy when it returns to its historical growth average, which you can find below:

As a reference, you will also find the returns of FEZ below, representative of the region, but only with a 9.53% exposure to Spain.

As you can see, the post-COVID rebound has been higher for Spain than for Europe, but on longer time frames the returns of a diversified European investment are still above. The post-market COVID was exceptional, and the risk now is that we return to markets similar to what we had before, where the outperformance of Spain was not here. Coupled with the recent price surge, I don’t see EWP as a buy right now.

Portfolio

EWP is one of the most concentrated ETFs I ever encountered. The portfolio is made up of only 20 companies, and the top 3 alone account for almost 50% of the fund: Banco Santander SA (SAN) at 18.14%, Iberdrola SA (OTCPK:IBDSF) at 13.87%, and Banco Bilbao Vizcaya Argentaria SA (BBVA) at 12.75%. The top 10 account for 75.58%. This high concentration has been the reason behind the recent explosive growth because high concentration means both high upside potential and high downside potential. We just saw the upside, I don’t want to see the downside. The portfolio mainly consists of large-cap companies, but not only: 6.46% of the fund is invested in mid-caps.

This is a very unique positioning, and very different from the one of FEZ, with a top three accounting for 17.89% of the fund and a top 10 accounting for 40.34% of the fund. FEZ holds 50 companies.

The fund’s sectoral split, representative of the 3, is mainly focused on financials (41.92%), utilities (20.72%), and industrials (14.15%). Again, not very diversified and with limited exposure to sectors like tech and healthcare. The sectoral split reflects the ongoing dynamic in Spain: a strong banking sector with global names and a focus on the power grid.

The Big Three

  • Banco Santander SA: 18.14%

Santander is the largest holding of the fund and a key driver of performance, as the stock returned a YTD performance of +93.05%. This strong upside has been driven by robust net interest income and cost cutting.

The Spanish 10 year rate is keeping a high level, and increased by 9% over the last year, supporting the net interest income of the bank to be high for a while.

The company also has a strong pan-European and Latin American exposure, slightly mitigating EWP’s Spanish focus with foreign sources of growth, but also volatility from emerging economies such as Brazil and Mexico.

  • Iberdrola SA: 13.87%

The second biggest holding of the fund is a global leader in renewable energy, with growth boosted by Spain's ambitious sustainability goals, and by European decarbonization plans. The company also has long-term contracted cash flows, making the company attractive, but the YTD performance is only 13.66%.

However, regulations are weighing on the company with the windfall tax extended to energy companies in 2025 by the Spanish government, putting the short- to medium-term earning perspectives at risk, and this is why this stock’s upside appears limited. A hold on EWP also is a good thing to wait for this uncertainty to go away.

  • Banco Bilbao Vizcaya Argentaria SA: 12.75%

The company's YTD performance is 74.09% for reasons very similar to those of Santander: increased net interest margins and cost dynamics. However, the company is more exposed to Latin American economies. Part of the growth is also coming from the ramping up of the digital banking activity, strengthening the book quality.

Financial Metrics

The financial metrics of EWP are showing conflicting considerations. Investing in EWP comes with a premium: the fund’s expense ratio is high at 0.50%, because EWP is the only alternative. Investing in Europe with FEZ will only cost 0.29%. This difference might come from the size difference: $1.4Bn for EWP vs. $4.6 Bn for ZEF, and the portfolio turnover: 15% for EWP, almost 3x higher than FEZ’s 6%. The liquidity is good, both funds have a median bid/ask spread of 0.02%.

Making sense with the sectoral split, EWP’s dividend yield is high: 2.66%, and higher than FEZ at 2.39%. However, if we look at the 30-day SEC yield, it is only 1.92% for EWP, suggesting that the dividend distribution might not be very sustainable.

If we look at valuation metrics, because EWP is focused on traditionally more defensive sectors than the average, the fund’s P/E is lower than its European peer: 13.0 vs. 16.2. However, the lower valuation doesn’t come with lower risks, as the fund’s standard deviation is higher: 19.50% (3 years), 22.87% (5 years), and 21.45% (10 years) against 18.37% (3 years), 20.74% (5 years), and 19.07% (10 years) for FEZ.

The volatility of EWP is higher; this naturally comes from the low level of diversification vs. FEZ, but doesn’t translate into a higher maximum drawdown. Over the last 5 years it was -32.29% for EWP, marginally lower than FEZ’s 32.33%.

We discussed the performance earlier, but not the risk-adjusted performance. The Sharpe ratio of the fund is 1.37 (3 years), 0.75 (5 years), and 0.35 (10 years) against 1.02 (3 years), 0.53 (5 years), and 0.40 (10 years) for FEZ. As you can see, the recent time frame metrics are very different from the ones with longer time frames, suggesting that if a return to the mean is to happen, the movement would be violent, an additional reason to be cautious when considering EWP.

Momentum or Rear-View Mirror?

The Spanish banking industry, at the center of EWP’s performance with fast-growing stocks and a 41.92% allocation, has been surging thanks to interest rates, margin expansion, and a resilient housing market. However, the industry is showing signs of caution as interest rates are at peak levels and economic growth is slowing down. Moreover, from here, the net interest income is only projected to grow at a 2.76% CAGR until 2030, which is limited given the explosive growth that the market is pricing.

With Iberdrola as a main holding, utilities remain an attractive sector because of the strong green initiatives in Europe, but a lot of uncertainty is around legislation and trade agreements. In Spain, the outlook is strong because of the energy market, but I don’t expect significant growth by 2026 because of the underlying regulatory uncertainty.

Industrial companies such as infrastructure providers and logistics showed strong recovery dynamics post-COVID and thanks to large infrastructure investments in Europe. However, concerns remain over the country’s high-interest rates that are delaying capital-intensive projects, the inflation going back up (while remaining at acceptable levels), and decreasing exports that are weighing on earning expectations for industrial activities.

Overall, I believe that in all of the key sectors of EWP, the tailwinds are diminishing, and the headwinds are reminding us that they are still here. After the blatant optimism of the markets, the return to reality could be violent. To answer the section’s title question, I will say rearview mirror.

Risks

The main risk from EWP is the correction risk, and this is the first thing I mentioned in this analysis. I will not elaborate further. I think you got the point. The second one is the concentration risk, and I also think I mentioned it enough for you to realize that there is absolutely no diversification when it comes to EWP. Now the combination of these two risks is a dangerous cocktail, and these two alone are reason enough not to buy EWP now.

Company-specific risks are to be considered, especially about the top 3, because the lack of diversification means that an underperformance of one of these 3 would have direct consequences on the fund’s returns. I believe that for Iberdrola, it is mainly about regulation, as renewable energy companies are overly reliant on government programs, and this is a key topic in Spain. For the two other banking companies, think about interest rates, how long will they stay at current levels, is the government going to do something about the high level of gains to redistribute to the economy, etc.? Overall, there is more downside to be considered here than upside events.

There is currency risk to be monitored with the Euro. The EUR/USD pair is now very beneficial to Euro denominated earnings converted to dollars, but this dynamic could change. The EUR/USD pair is now trading at 1.178, up 6% over the last year, partly explaining EWP’s upside. Overall, projections are that the Euro will strengthen in the medium term, with no clear consensus on short-term movements.

Conclusion

I recommend a hold on EWP, as most of the upside has been realized (and very quickly), and risk remains elevated. The smart thing to do now is to wait for a price correction and to clear the risks I mentioned in this analysis to approach this ETF with more certainty and a favorable risk/reward ratio.

data.title: Porsche AG: Between Ferrari Dreams And VW Reality
data.description:
Introduction

You already know from the title that this article will focus on the leading German luxury automaker, Porsche AG (OTCPK:DRPRY) (OTCPK:DRPRF). Allow me to begin with a personal anecdote.

A few days ago, I was taking my kids to kindergarten by bike. While we were crossing a street, my oldest boy shouted, "Look, Dad, there is a Porsche." As the car approached, he looked better and had to correct himself, saying somewhat disappointingly, "Oh no, it's only an Audi." I think you get the irony, and by the way, you should know that we currently own a Volkswagen, so we are one further step down in what I would call the German car "food chain."

Now, my kid is 4, and his brand perception was quite accurate, embodying both Porsche's attractiveness and its problem. Porsche is a brand that immediately sparks enthusiasm and attracts customers. Its cars are synonymous with luxury and speed, which positions them above most other vehicles. At the same time, they are not so unique and exclusive that they are immediately identifiable. So, my kid mistook an Audi for a Porsche. He would not have done so with a Ferrari, which, in the worst case, could be mistaken for a Lamborghini.

I don't want to rely too much on anecdotes. After all, if you have followed me on Seeking Alpha, you know that I am fundamental and macro-driven. But I wanted to share it because it quickly portrays the reason why Porsche's stock performance has been a disaster since its IPO in 2023. Volkswagen needed cash to fund its energy transition and therefore sold a small stake of the company to the public in the hope it would mimic Ferrari's public market success.

However, although the quality of its cars, its engineering, and its brand reputation position it above most premium automakers, Porsche still shares with the industry two main characteristics: it runs a volume-driven business, which is also sensitive to the different geographies and the local market dynamics. In other words, Porsche manufactures and sells over 300k vehicles and has often relied, at least in the past two decades, on the growing Chinese market. Ferrari, on the other hand, sells 14k cars a year and intentionally keeps its volumes under control to preserve and even enhance its exclusivity. This allows it to pick the markets it wants to address. For example, in recent years, it has reduced the number of cars it sells in China because its exclusivity wasn't perceived as much as elsewhere. As a result, Ferrari wasn't happy with the margins there and simply reduced the number it sold in China, redirecting those volumes to other markets such as North America. Ferrari could do this without damaging (but, actually, improving) its financials because, in many other areas of the world, there are customers who are willing to wait two or three years for their first car with a prancing horse on the hood. In the meantime, Ferraris will become scarcer in China, helping the local wealthy consumer perceive and appreciate better how exclusive it is to own and drive a car manufactured in Maranello, Italy.

I won't hide: at first, I, too, thought Porsche could offer decent upside after its IPO, even though I didn't think it would be another Ferrari. But, yes, I bought a bit of the stock and held it for some months. In the meantime, I realized my thesis was wrong as I saw deteriorating fundamentals and challenging macroeconomic factors, which made me change my initial thesis and sell the stock at a loss of around 10%. In hindsight, I had not spotted a major shift in China's consumer preferences, which we will consider in a moment.

Porsche's Latest Financials

Porsche, as we have said, addresses high-end customers. At the same time, these customers are usually not necessarily ultra-high-net-worth individuals. This implies that, even though they are well-off and fare well, their spending on cars is still somewhat cyclical and discretionary. As a matter of fact, many Porsches are sold through financing, which means their drivers are not truly their owners, at least not yet. With a global environment where interest rates are high pretty much everywhere, financing a Porsche has therefore become more expensive. The opposite is also true; with interest rates projected to come down, Porsche could see a favorable headwind materialize.

But, leaving aside some exogenous circumstances, Porsche has managed its transition to electric vehicles perhaps too quickly, shifting heavily its portfolio towards electrified vehicles. This brought along huge investments, which created a double issue when we considered that BEVs were not meeting consumers' favor as expected, and when we think that they are usually margin-dilutive. Now, at the end of H1 2025, Porsche's deliveries in Europe are 56.6% electrified, with 36.2% being battery-electric vehicles and another 20.4% being hybrid solutions. In particular, the Macan BEV is seeing terrific success, accounting for a third of the model's sales. However, BEV sales damage margins and can cannibalize higher-margin ICE sales.

This explains why the first six months of the year were not an easy game for Porsche. The group's sales decreased 6.7% to EUR 18.2B, with the RoS compressing by over 10 percentage points, moving from 15.7% at the end of H1 2024 to 5.5%. When we talk about the risk of cannibalization, here we have an interesting example. The company's operating profit plunged 67% to only EUR 1B. However, the automotive EBITDA margin is still in the mid-teens, standing at 16%. Nonetheless, a year ago, it was 24.1%.

When we look at the automotive cash flow, we see that it was down 64.7% to only EUR 400M.

All of this led to Porsche's releasing a FY25 outlook, which revised downward the guidance to a RoS of 5%, with a 2 percentage point drag coming from U.S. tariffs. Overall, the company expects its revenues to be in the range of EUR 37B to EUR 38B.

Let's put it in Porsche's words: "The entire 2025 is significantly shaped by a strategic realignment." In other words, the company's management is not expecting that the second half of the year will see Porsche's performance rebound.

In the last earnings call held in July, Porsche's management pointed out that record sales in North America were a positive sign. However, they had to acknowledge that, margin-wise, it was the bloodbath we had seen. Another positive trend is the growing demand for exclusive options, which is making Porsche follow the path that Ferrari began to trace a few years ago, and that made its profits explode. However, China kept reporting weakness, with volumes down 50% from the peak, and the luxury segment was shrinking. As a side note, we should recall what I have previously highlighted. There is a cultural shift in China that is pushing towards being less ostentatious with luxury items. It doesn't look good to show openly what one can afford to buy, and high-end cars immediately suffered the effect of this change. It is a trend that is called "quiet luxury," and more and more consumers choose to avoid flashy cars and luxury items. This shift goes along with a renewed preference for Chinese brands, which is a patriotic shift that we can understand, given the geopolitical tensions around the world. So, if public display of luxury cars is now heading in a muted direction, the environment for Porsche becomes more challenging. Add the threat of domestic competition from Chinese brands, and I think you will agree with me that it is unlikely to see easing pressure on Porsche soon. Porsche's management's view, as expressed in the remarks during the last earnings call, confirms that next year should bring the first benefits of product and marketing realignment. At the same time, Porsche deemed that it is too early to commit to a double-digit margin by next year. This leads us to the valuation.

Porsche's Valuation

Although the stock seems to have found a bottom, we have to consider that, if its guidance is maintained, Porsche is basically reporting the margins of lower-tier automakers with GM, which will probably beat its RoS. Right now, the stock trades at a FWD P/E of 28, which surely accounts for low future earnings, but also suggests that investors are expecting a recovery that will rapidly compress the multiple. However, I don't see any macroeconomic or organic tailwind that will lead the company's earnings to increase by almost 50% in one year, which is what analysts are expecting. Part of the earnings upside is linked to temporarily depressed net income because of extraordinary costs such as tariffs, restructuring plans, and battery impairments. But some growth is needed to meet this consensus. With China so weak, the company will highly depend on North America's performance. If the forecasts are, on the other hand, met or exceeded, the stock will see its earnings multiple compress to 12.4 by the end of 2027, which would make Porsche quite interesting.

When we look at the FWD EV/EBITDA ratio, we see an interesting 7, reflecting that Porsche's EBITDA is still relatively strong, as we have seen above. This means that, with a different perspective, Porsche could be a value pick that has some upside.

Nonetheless, its FCF right now is unstable, and in this environment, it is hard to predict whether it will resume sustained growth in the next few years. Moreover, we are still in a financial deterioration streak, and deliveries are shrinking. BEV sales are growing, but they are margin-dilutive. Right now, Porsche doesn't justify luxury multiples anymore, which means that it will converge towards Volkswagen rather than closing the multiple with Ferrari.

Buying Porsche stock requires high conviction that EV margins will increase and China will rebound. I see both as unlikely in the near term.

Is Porsche then a sell? It could be if it were to lose its brand premium permanently, which might be a bit too harsh right now.

This is why I consider the best rating right now a hold. Porsche is in a sort of limbo: it is not cheap enough to be a true value play, but it is not even so weak that we should just dump it. It is better to hold and wait for the FY2025 results and then see if there are signs of margin stabilization and sales rebound. For now, it is better to deploy our cash elsewhere, where the outlook is clearer and the valuation more attractive.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Rithm Capital And Paramount: Ascendant Asset Manager Enters Booming Manhattan Office Real Estate
data.description:
Rithm Capital (NYSE:RITM) is building an incredibly well-diversified platform of adjacent yet complementary businesses that is set to transform the internally managed mortgage REIT. The recent acquisitions, rather than being distractions as implied by bears, will push RITM beyond its core residential mortgage origination and servicing business into an ascendant asset manager with a growing base of fee-earning assets under management ("AUM") and now with a portfolio of Class A office real estate in Manhattan, the best office real estate market in America. I'm bullish, last covering RITM with a buy rating at the start of 2024. RITM is pushing to break out of the mould of a highly leveraged cookie-cutter residential mREIT with an elevated dividend yield and extreme economic sensitivity. The $1.6 billion acquisition of Paramount Group (NYSE:PGRE) this week and the acquisition of Crestline Management, an alternative investment manager with $17 billion in AUM, all point to a pursuit of growth and sustained dividend yield compression.

RITM last declared a quarterly cash dividend of $0.25 per share, kept unchanged from its prior distribution, and $1 per share annualized for an 8.14% dividend yield. This has compressed from 9.5% when I last covered RITM. But the distributions are below their level before the pandemic. Yield compression is the prize being chased by RITM, reflecting an increase in the value of its underlying equity base. To be clear, there is a reason the market has priced Camden Property Trust (CPT) at a yield just under 4% and Arbor Realty Trust (ABR) at a yield just over 10%. Both are REITs, both are involved in multifamily. However, ABR invests in debt, and CPT invests in physical real estate. This means a vastly different risk profile. Hence, the acquisition of PGRE is crucial. It's a tool for yield compression, being closed as demand for office space in the borough booms and its vacancy rates continue to dip on the back of positive office absorption.

Paramount Group, Class A Office, And Manhattan's Fundamentals

PGRE owned and managed a large portfolio formed from 17 properties in Manhattan and San Francisco. This was spread across 13.1 million square feet, with Manhattan driving 71% of its wholly owned square feet of total Class A office space of 11.6 million. The is another 700,000 square feet of wholly owned retail and theater space as of the end of the fiscal 2025 second quarter. PGRE also has an investment management business where it serves as a general partner for a number of real estate funds.

Manhattan's leasing is booming with activity totaling 6.99 million square feet in the second quarter, 33% above its five-year quarterly average. Net office absorption was also positive at 2.17 million square feet to ramp up the year-to-date total to 5.17 million square feet. This was the highest total since the start of the millennium. Return-to-office mandates, Trump's financial deregulatory agenda, Fed rate cuts, and heightened deal-making and IPO activity are set to form drivers for continued office space demand. Strong net absorption came with the borough's sublease availability rate dipping by 20 basis points year-over-year to 3.4% at the end of the second quarter.

Manhattan's total vacancy rate dipped by 130 basis points year-over-year to 15.2% as of the end of July, far below the U.S. national vacancy rate of 19.4%. There are only two other cities in the U.S. with lower vacancy rates, Miami and Los Angeles. But Miami's vacancy rate is worsening, and Los Angeles has a listing rate that is around 40% lower than Manhattan's. Critically, the pandemic and an elevated Fed funds rate have markedly reduced the pipeline of new office properties being constructed, helping push new and renewal leasing spreads higher. For PGRE, this was 2.6% on a GAAP basis, albeit negative 5.4% on a cash basis, and with the REIT's same-store leased occupancy dipping by 80 basis points sequentially to end at 85.4%. A significant cohort of PGRE's tenants are in the financial industry, including JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM), Morgan Stanley (MS), and PIMCO owner Allianz (OTCPK:ALIZF).

Manhattan-focused office REITs Vornado Realty (VNO) and SL Green (SLG) were in the running for PGRE, with stock prices that are up 238% and 216% respectively, from their 2023 lows. Manhattan-based private equity giant Blackstone (BX) was also in the running for PGRE. This was not a rogue acquisition, but a positioning in the best office real estate market in the U.S. just as the Fed begins a rate-cutting cycle. San Francisco is more challenged, with a vacancy rate of 23.2% as of July, higher than the national average, but with leasing ticking up.

PGRE shareholders are being paid $6.60 per share by RITM, with the deal set to close in the fourth quarter of 2025. PGRE held no other outstanding public securities. The deal is a positive, at a premium to the REIT's 52-week lows and providing an exit for investors that had not received a dividend since the summer of 2024. PGRE suspended its quarterly cash dividend in lieu of share buybacks. Critically, RITM will assume total debt of $3.68 billion that was on PGRE's balance sheet, including $660 million in cash, cash equivalents, and restricted cash.

PGRE's free cash flow ("FCF") should improve on rate cuts, with this figure at $68.21 million as of the end of the second quarter. It could also move higher on post-acquisition organisational changes as PGRE had an elevated executive compensation program. RITM is set to conduct business through four operating segments; (1) Origination and Servicing; (2) Investment Portfolio; (3) Residential Transitional Lending; and (4) Asset Management. The mREIT should hold $2.5 billion to $3 billion of available cash and liquidity following the closing of the deal. RITM has four outstanding preferreds that have all seen intense yield compression since 2023. I'd be choosing the equity here over the 7.50% Series A Fixed/Float Preferred (NYSE:RITM.PR.A), the 7.125% Series B Fixed/Float Preferred (NYSE:RITM.PR.B), the 6.375% Series C Fixed/Float Preferred (NYSE:RITM.PR.C), and the 7.00% Reset Rate Series D Cumulative Preferred Stock (NYSE:RITM.PR.D).

Conclusion

RITM's earnings available for distribution ("EAD") was $0.54 per share for the second quarter, up 2 cents sequentially and beating consensus by the same amount. The mREIT's asset management business saw AUM end the quarter at $36 billion, up by $1 billion from the first quarter as Newrez, its mortgage origination and servicing platform, generated a 19% pretax return on equity. Offices are not going anywhere; they're essentially modern factories in a modern knowledge-led economy. Manhattan is the undisputed financial capital of the world, beating out London, Paris, Milan, and Hong Kong to be where capital is created and managed from all aspects of the U.S. and global economy. The economics of agglomeration describes how urban clusters form, and Manhattan's strategic moat that will drive continued demand for office space as the economy grows. Bears have flagged risks from pending Mayoral changes, but the floated rent freeze concerns only stabilized apartments. RITM is a buy on what could be sustained yield compression as the market prices the mREIT as an asset manager and Class A office owner with highly recurring earnings.

data.title: FinVolution: A Compelling GARP Play In Asia
data.description:
Thesis

I rate FinVolution (NYSE:FINV) as a 'Strong Buy,' while I expect 62% upside for the next 12 months based on reasonable assumptions. This thesis is built on three pillars: growth, valuation, and risk acknowledgment.

Regarding growth, the first pillar, they increased 96% Y/Y in their number of international customers, which is significant. While net revenues from the Chinese segment are growing at 10% Y/Y, the international segment (Indonesia and the Philippines) is growing at 74% Y/Y (last presentation, p. 9). Additionally, they are expanding into Pakistan. I know that 'investing in Pakistan' may be a controversial topic for some. But keep in mind two things. First, Pakistan has 251 million people, the fifth most populous country in the world. What's more, it's one of the youngest ones, with 64% of people under 30 years old, and has very low fintech penetration. In other words, this means a lot of room for FinVolution to grow there. Second, the Pakistani stock index soared 88% in USD in 2024, which was the second-best-performing country. I understand that some people avoid riskier environments; I often do, too. But we should remember that riskier investments correlate with 'juicier' returns. Whether we agree or disagree a priori, it's wise to analyze the evidence and arguments first before reaching a definitive conclusion.

Moving on to the second and third pillars, valuation and risk, it's clear that the company is undervalued, even when using different models. However, nothing is 'undervalued' for no reason. That's when the risk becomes a major factor. The main risk factor is regulatory and political. This aspect is further divided into three sub-aspects. First, the delisting risk from the US government. Second, the 'confiscation' risk by the Chinese government, in this case, may affect the ADR (American Deposit Receipt), so foreign investors would lose their rights to the profits. Third, stricter regulations in China that stifle the company's growth and profitability in its Chinese segment. The chances of delisting and confiscation seem pretty low to me, due to a game theory analysis. I'll delve deeper into these topics in the risk and conclusion sections.

Company Overview

FinVolution Group is a Chinese fintech that connects borrowers to lenders on its platform, so its revenue is derived from fees rather than accrued interest. They focus on end-users and on small companies. Lastly, they're expanding fast in other Asian countries such as Indonesia, the Philippines, and they are entering Pakistan.

Looking at the most recent results, we note several key points. First, most of their revenue comes from connecting lenders with borrowers and not from accruing interest. Second, operating and net margins when compared to 2022 are almost the same; in other words, we aren't noting either an improvement in efficiency or a worsening of it. The 'other revenue' segment includes customer referral fees and revenue generated from new businesses, according to the SEC filings, and as we note, despite being small, it is growing at a triple-digit rate.

Regarding their international share, things could not be better, as they stated the following in the last earnings call:

International transaction volume increased 39% Y/Y to RMB 3.2 billion, and loan balance rose 50% to RMB 2.1 billion. (...) Underpinning the growth is our expanding customer base. We onboarded 1.6 million new borrowers during the second quarter, a 96% Y/Y increase.

This growth rate in client numbers is even more bullish than it appears on the surface. HubSpot has this to say on the matter:

Brands have a 60-70% chance of making a sale to an existing customer, but that number drops to 20% for new customers.

My point here is that it's harder to acquire new clients than it is to sell to existing ones. So if they're having success in the hardest part, the easiest will be easier.

Regarding expanding into Pakistan, in the last earnings call, they stated the following:

And for our new markets such as Pakistan, after we get the NBFC license last year, we just recently secured a Buy Now, Pay Later license in July, and can make us the first fintech platform that can operate both online and offline, representing a powerful endorsement from the regulators.

I know that there are things that are not as nice as we would like in Pakistan, but let's consider the significant potential for fintech and credit growth. First, Pakistan is the 5th most populous country, with 241 million people. Second, it's one of the youngest countries, with 64% of the population younger than 30. Third, a very low banking penetration rate, since just 7% of people had a bank account. These factors create a fertile environment for the fintech sector to grow quickly, more than in other countries.

The fintech industry in Asia is expected to grow at a 16% CAGR, according to Mordor Intelligence, but other sources like Cognitive Market Research are even more bullish, forecasting a 21.2% CAGR between 2024 and 2031.

In June, they issued $150mm in convertible bonds. The two reasons they put forth are international expansion and lowering the cost of capital, which they label as 'strategic priorities.' But why issue debt while at the same time they buy back shares and pay dividends? Why don't they use that money to fund the company instead of returning it to the stockholders? The keyword here is 'lower cost of capital.' I explained in my article on Knife River how a company can increase its theoretical value by just increasing debt.

Let me share a quick example. Let's suppose a company has a cost of equity of 11% and a cost of debt of 5%.

Scenario 1: If the company has no debt (100% equity financed), the cost of capital [WACC] is 11%.

Scenario 2: If the company gets debt and now has 40% debt and 60% equity, in this case, its cost of capital [WACC] is 8.6%.

WACC = 0.4*0.05 + 0.6*0.11 = 0.086

In my opinion, it is very appealing to increase a bit of debt (not much). Keep in mind the ratio of total debt/assets was 0.1% before these bonds were issued, and now it's at 4.4%. Likewise, the total liabilities-to-assets ratio only increased from 35% to 37%. Anyway, this increase in debt has only a small impact on the company.

However, what I definitely don't like is that these bonds are 'convertible bonds.' They will dilute equity.

Regarding stock repurchases, the company has $115mm remaining to buy back under the last authorized program in March. This represents 10.7% of the current market cap. Also, the board approved a significant change in dividend policy, moving from a minimum of 10% of net profit to a new range of 20% to 30% annually.

Seeking Alpha's rating shift

Something that caught the eye of a few of us is the change in Seeking Alpha's Quant rating. It was a 'strong buy' until August 22, and then became a 'hold' on August 25. The primary reason for this change was a significant shift in EPS revisions. Six analysts revised their earnings estimate downward, and seven reduced their estimate for revenues.

However, they still are pretty bullish. Seven of them rate it as a strong buy, and one rates it as a buy. They expect a 47% upside. The 2025 EPS estimate went from $1.67 to $1.6, an implied reduction of 4.19%, and the 2026 and 2027 estimates were reduced by 4.8% and 4.7%, respectively. The consensus target price was reduced by just 3.9%. In other words, Seeking Alpha's quant rating appears to be very sensitive to the quantity of analysts revising upward/downward and not to the magnitude of the average revision.

Wrapping up, this change in revisions is not a big deal as of now.

Valuation

First, the forward price-to-earnings is 4.68x. While this may not appear cheap on a historical basis, as we note in the chart below, it suggests a strong undervaluation when compared to other stocks or the broader market.

I'm going to compare the relative valuation to its peers using Seeking Alpha's consumer finance industry. A look at the P/E and price-to-sales ratios shows the stock is undervalued. In fact, regression analysis suggests a 9.57 P/E ratio as fair value, which implies a potential upside of 104%. Similarly, a look into the second model shows a theoretical price-to-sales ratio of 2.08x, which implies an upside of 110% from the current valuation.

Keep in mind that management guidance for the 2025 fiscal year is expected to grow between 10 and 15% Y/Y.

From another angle, price-to-book is at 0.87x, while the median sector is 1.27x. This only suggests a potential upside of 45%. Now, when we analyze it combined with ROE, it shows a stronger undervaluation, with an astonishing upside of 135%.

Chinese companies can be very challenging to value from an absolute standpoint, and many avoid doing so. I think Karl Popper said something wise when he talked about the falsifiability of an argument. In absolute valuation, we're not trying to predict the future, but instead to explicitly state our assumptions and stress test them. After all, time will prove us right or wrong, but we will have feedback on why we're right or wrong.

I tried different absolute models for this stock, and by the numbers, they suggest a strong undervaluation, even with triple-digit upside. Let me share with you what I think are conservative and reasonable assumptions for this stock. If you disagree, let me know in the comments, and let's have a constructive discussion.

First, I assumed a declining growth rate in EPS from 15% Y/Y in the next year to 4.5% after the tenth year. This implies an EPS CAGR of 9.7%. Also, I assumed a 10% payout rate, which is very conservative, as they are planning to buy back 10% of the market cap and to distribute dividends roughly at 20-30% of earnings.

The Chinese 10-year rate is at 1.85%, but as we all know, Chinese stocks are riskier due to the highly centralized political system. So, this risk-free rate may not be representative and appropriate for this valuation. The beta is 0.63x according to TradingView, and other betas, like Finviz, report a beta of 0.43x. This is one of the reasons that I'm somewhat skeptical of the CAPM model and certain technical analysis tools. The average cost of equity for a US company is between 7% and 11%. FinVolution has a low debt risk, a low operating risk, and a high regulatory and political risk, so I believe that 13% is reasonable for a cost of equity, considering these three factors.

For obvious reasons, I cannot say that the company will grow at 9.7% per year for the next years, it may be more or less than that. Also, the discount rate is very challenging to address. If calculated in the standard way, it would be as follows. The Chinese 10-year risk-free is at 1.85%, the beta is at 0.63, and the equity risk premium is at 6%. That gives us a discount rate of 5.63%.

Cost of Equity = 0.0185 + (0.63*0.06) = 5.63%

Obviously, it is nonsense to use this rate to discount a Chinese company like FinVolution. However, to assign a 13% rate can also be a bit arbitrary, even though I already gave my reasons for that. Anyway, a sensitivity analysis may help us to see a range (forest) and not rigid assumptions (trees).

Risks

The main risk is regulatory. They are a Chinese company and operate mainly in the Chinese market; therefore, they are subject to the government's potential changes in regulations. The CFO addressed the new round of regulations that will become effective on October 1. He stated that there may be 'some near-term volatility ahead,' but in the long term, these regulations may be positive for FinVolution, while the 'long tail is being cleaned out' of the market. In other words, he expects that these new regulations will reduce competition, especially for riskier competitors. Also, keep in mind the structure of this company; my peer provided a solid overview of this in his article. In short, FINV is an ADR (American deposit receipt) of a shell company or VIE (Variable Interest Entity) in a Cayman Islands-based company. This VIE company doesn't own the operating Chinese company directly; instead, it owns the rights to its profits.

Second, while Chinese credit is growing fast, it is not as fast as a couple of years ago. I covered this in depth in my article on Qfin Holdings (QFIN). By the way, while I was somewhat between Buy and Hold, I'm walking slowly towards the bullish camp of Qfin. However, I'm a convinced bull for FinVolution for two reasons: first, the overseas expansion, and second, there were some concerns raised by the short seller attack on Qfin, which dissuaded me a little. Going back to the Chinese credit, in short, it's showing a declining trend. In 2015, total loans were growing at 15.4%; in 2023, at 10.6%; and now they're growing at 6.8% Y/Y. Now you may understand why I'm more bullish on FinVolution than Qfin; the international expansion mitigates these declining growth rates.

Third, competition is strong. While the fintech industry is somewhat fragmented, according to Mordor Intelligence, it is already on a trend of market consolidation.

Fourth, the growth story for this company relies on international expansion. This has additional risks to consider, especially in Pakistan, where the political factor can be more complex.

The last and fifth risk is potential currency devaluation. The dollar index (DXY) is oversold on both a daily and weekly basis. While I'm not stating that the US dollar will necessarily rise just due to this oversold condition, we should be aware of it. Regarding these four currencies, the Chinese Yuan, Indonesian Rupiah, Philippine Peso, and Pakistani Rupee are high-yield (weak) currencies. If the US dollar, which is a safe-haven currency, rises, they will suffer. Also, I'd like to emphasize that this last one has a negligible impact on the stock for now. But in my opinion, we should take seriously the Pakistani expansion, since the metrics in the early stages are critical as they may strongly influence the management and the market's perception of the stock.

Conclusion

While there are some political and regulatory risks to consider, I believe that those catastrophic scenarios are unlikely. I prefer to base my thesis on the most probable scenarios while keeping those risks in mind. To conclude, there are two frameworks that can be useful to make a decision on this stock.

First, we may consider the political risks through game theory. I won't get too deep into this, but in short, I don't see enough incentives for the governments to do wild changes such as massive de-listings. Yes, I expect that we will continue seeing what we're already seeing, i.e., tariffs (due to the current account deficits) and stricter regulations in China. However, we should keep in mind that China benefits from Western investments. While they will put tariffs and will control the 'sovereignty' of Chinese companies, at the same time, they don't have incentives to scare off foreign investors. In other words, even though the Chinese government is very nationalistic, foreign investors would be seen as allies who help by investing in that nation. Wrapping up, from a Nash equilibrium perspective, confiscation, delisting, and any form of 'political chaos' are highly unlikely.

Second, from a heuristics perspective, we should separate what we know from what we don't know. There are things I wouldn't be able to know for sure. I cannot predict the tails of the distribution; for example, a regime change, or a new irrational government doing irrational things (irrational in a game theory context). This is what I already know. This company is growing and has more room to grow for a decade at least. As I already discussed in the risk section, when comparing it to Qfin, I see FinVolution as an 'Asian' company, and not as a Chinese company; therefore, the growth rates may be higher and for longer. The expansion into Pakistan can be seen as a call option, where the downside risk is limited and the potential upside can be stellar. Relative and absolute valuation suggest significant undervaluation, even taking into account the 'fuzziness' of the cost of equity calculation. I'm basing my thesis on what I know.

Having said that, with its pros and cons, I believe that the pros significantly outweigh the cons. That's why I'm giving it a strong buy rating.

Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best GARP Idea investment competition, which runs through September 23. With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!

data.title: Alamos Gold: Hidden Value Unlocked After Recent Deal, Still Undervalued
data.description:
Introduction

In my previous coverage of Alamos Gold (NYSE:AGI) (TSX:AGI:CA), I talked about the company’s impressive growth pipeline, with plans to increase gold output by about 50% from 2025 to 2028 while maintaining or even lowering an exceptionally low AISC thanks to their high-quality operations in Canada backed by solid financials.

Now, the company has made some great advancements that the market seems to be ignoring, with most of the ~20% upside seen since publishing the article being attributable to the higher gold prices, and I’d like to update the thesis to include the recent developments.

Recent Quarter

Following the Q2 report, the company remains on track to achieve their full-year production guidance (580,000 to 630,000 ounces), although they recently increased their cost guidance, with the CEO saying:

As a result of higher-than-budgeted share price compensation and royalty expense through the first half of the year and a slower start at Magino & Young-Davidson, we are revising our 2025 cost guidance. Full year all-in sustaining costs are expected to be 12% higher than our original guidance with approximately 40% of that increase attributable to external factors. From a cost perspective, our first half performance was not reflective of our long-term track record of meeting or exceeding expectations nor is it reflective of our strong outlook. (...) We had -- we got off to a slow start this year. We had very difficult conditions in Canada this year, that affected both our operations at YD and Island Gold.

Still, the AISC remains very solid in this market, and I believe that they have significant potential even if it doesn’t fall as they expect (for reference, they estimated an AISC of $1,175/oz in 2027 as investments go down and they reap the benefits of their projects in development).

Their free cash flow also improved significantly in Q2 from the -$20 million seen in Q1, to $85 million despite the higher costs (AISC is higher YoY but decreased 18% QoQ), with both better FCF and better AISC being expected in H2.

As they say, 2025 and 2026 won’t be the best years in terms of cash flows due to the higher CAPEX as they work on bringing their significant project pipeline into production, but the real potential could be very significant from 2027, driven by the completion of these projects and lower CAPEX as a result. At current gold prices, we could easily be looking at even more than $750 million in free cash flow by 2028 (at $3,300/oz, they say they’re able to make over $600 million by 2027), which is not bad at all for a ~$13.28 billion market cap given the company’s quality. As I mention below, this is one of the highest-quality gold miners on the planet, not some random miner with failing operations in high-risk areas, so it’s normal to have to pay a premium for it.

Looking at them from a longer-term perspective, we see very significant growth in virtually everything, including per share numbers which are very important. With the new projects coming over the next few years, the compounding potential in this environment remains very impressive. As a note, assuming ~$500 million in CAPEX in 2025 (more than the annualized number from H1), combined with their $959 million in operating cash flow before working capital changes, we’re looking at about $459 million in FCF (again, before working capital changes).

They also filed for a $500M mixed securities shelf, which they plan to use for general corporate purposes and developments. I believe issuing stock in this environment with such high gold prices is generally a good idea, as there’s nothing that guarantees that they’ll reach what we estimate to be a fair value before the price of gold falls back. I remain bullish on gold in the long term, but that doesn’t mean it can’t crash in the near-term.

Another thing that I mentioned in the analysis was their Turkish operations. They’ve been in a conflict with the Turkish government for some years and even filed a lawsuit for more than $1 billion, claiming "expropriation and unfair and inequitable treatment" under an investment treaty between the Netherlands and Turkey (as Alamos has Dutch subsidiaries). However, in a very recent development, they sold their Turkish subsidiary for $470 million, which I believe is an excellent development that unlocks a lot of potential.

Having written down these assets years ago (although I’m not sure if the market was assigning 0 value to these operations, but they definitely weren’t in the spotlight), I even said before that “the way I view this is that I don't think anything bad can come from this, as anything they can recover is better than 0” - and $470 million ($160 million, $160 million, and $150 million in three stages over the two years after the closing) is certainly amazing for a ~$13.2 billion market cap. That’s especially good since they invested over $250 million in their Turkish mining projects after acquiring the assets for $40 million in 2010, so we’re potentially looking at a nice profit without even mining. As they say in the press release, this money is expected to be used to:

(...) pay down existing debt and fund the development of its ongoing growth projects. These include the Phase 3+ Expansion at the Island Gold District, the Lynn Lake Project, and the Puerto Del Aire project in Mexico.

So, not a bad outcome overall from something that was not even operational and at this point was probably nearly forgotten or worth close to nothing by the market since we’re looking at issues lasting for years at this point. This goes to show how even this type of project that may be hidden at first unless you dig a bit to find them can hold immense value for the right buyer at the right time. As a note, Alamos has slides regarding this project, but others may not include this type of mine in their presentations.

Again, the company remains similar to Agnico Eagle Mines (AEM), both of them being a top pick for gold mining investors looking for high-quality assets with very low AISC, excellent mine life (~20 years on average for Alamos’ Canadian assets), and good management teams. I’d even argue that Alamos is better overall thanks to their massive organic growth pipeline, excellent costs, and the possibility of being bought by companies like AEM that would want to expand their portfolio with excellent low-cost assets in safe jurisdictions.

Macro Changes

As said in other recent gold mining articles, I believe most of the stock’s recent jump generally has very little to do with the company itself in this case, and is more likely just the effect of gold’s new ATH. Again, when the factors that gold is used as a hedge against become more and more likely, we shouldn’t be surprised when gold itself sees jumps like the ~88.5% recorded over the past 5 years or ~40% YTD. This is the effect of many factors “boiling together” for several years, and now we see the results in a time when the market may rush to hedge against them.

In my opinion, the ultimate reason behind gold’s recent jump was rate cut expectations, especially after we’ve seen Powell’s Jackson Hole remarks and all the recent data released, which were seen as very bullish for gold since they made a September cut very likely, as we can see. As I said in the SSR Mining (SSRM) update, if done right and not rushed to a point where it can shock the markets, rate cuts have a significant boosting effect on the economy thanks to lowering the cost of debt, which in turn leads to more transactions, investments, higher discretionary budgets, etc. - this is ultimately their goal and the reason why this tool is used this way. However, they also push money into other assets, including ones outside of the US, and also into commodities, which can then continue to weaken the dollar against other assets/currencies, which would be a double-win for gold mining stocks, at least. Adding Trump’s tariffs and all the uncertainty seen at the same time, it’s really not something incredible that gold prices rose so much lately.

Plus, another thing I also noted in the B2Gold (BTG) updated analysis posted recently is that Tether has been developing quite a bit into gold mining after recently buying more exposure into other commodities (especially food/farm-related), which I believe could be another potential to bring attention to gold and gold mining stocks, alongside other commodities if they continue. I believe that we could be looking at a potentially new source of demand coming from crypto buyers, who generally don’t have much in common with gold investors. After all, Tether has a massive influence in the crypto sector, with their stablecoins ultimately helping shape the crypto market into what it is today by providing a very important or even fundamental asset in the industry.

This can build some common ground between crypto and gold, and especially bring more attention to gold (not so much vice versa), which continued to breach its all-time high and could get into FOMO, hype, and other market phenomena (especially if we believe how prone crypto buyers tend to be to these), especially if inflation/hard landing/recession/etc. fears rise - which are part of why I think gold’s upside potential is nearly unlimited, especially when we consider its unique sources of demand (jewelry/investing/banks representing ~90% of the demand). Also, as mentioned, earlier this year, Tether expanded their stake in my largest position - Adecoagro (AGRO) - from ~20% to ~70% at prices much higher than today, which I believe is a very interesting development that could shine a new light on commodities going forward. It may be too early now, but I believe there is potential to see a “Tether effect” on commodities in the future if they keep this up.

Be Mindful Of Risks

Still, no company is perfect, and there are still risks at the end of the day. Besides the significant exposure to gold and concentration on Canada, both of which can lead to significant volatility or pressure, there's also execution risk.

For example, they wouldn't be the first company to overpromise and underdeliver, and I'm particularly worried about their ability to reach their longer-term AISC targets ($1,175/oz by 2027) in an environment that would benefit gold (e.g., higher inflation, which also affects their production costs), especially if there's some kind of delay. This would then have an effect on the valuation, as the market is likely pricing in a certain production at a certain time.

Plus the environmental risks associated with Canada's weather, which can generate higher costs and even lead to production downtime. For example, Hudbay Minerals (HBM) and SSR Mining, which I covered the other day, even closed their mines back in June due to the 2025 wildfires in Manitoba and Saskatchewan. This comes after wildfires led to other mines being closed in 2024 as well (such as Champion Iron (OTCQX:CIAFF)'s) - and winters aren't that friendly either.

Updated Valuation

I’ll start with a theoretical $400 million FCF in 2025 this time to account for the higher gold prices (~13% lower than the assumption made above based on their expectations as of September; also accounting for potential working capital changes and other unknown events). Again, I’ll assume a growth rate that leads to a 50% increase in FCF by 2028, backed by the increase in production, meaning ~14.47% CAGR from 2025’s level to 2028, followed by a 4.5% CAGR for the remaining period until year 10 if they manage to keep up the expansion with new projects/acquisitions and a 2.5% terminal growth rate plus a WACC of 7.5% (assumptions: ~9% cost of equity, ~6.5% cost of debt). With 420.47 million shares outstanding and $97.9 million in net cash, discounting the cash flows and terminal value gives an enterprise value of $16.79 billion, which, after adding the net cash, implies an equity value of $16.89 billion, or ~$40.18 per share.

And that’s without even adding the $470 million from the Turkish mine sale. Simply adding that to the equity value (similar effect to repaying the debt and holding the rest of the cash, without investing it), that’s a price of $41.30, although they definitely have the potential to do more with it.

Conclusions

Overall, Alamos remains a buy thanks to their very solid growth pipeline, high-quality and low-cost assets, and great balance sheet and management team, being positioned for a great future even if gold prices fall. Again, this is one of the highest-quality gold miners out there, and when we add the organic growth potential and 18-20 years of current average mine life plus the falling AISC once their growth projects become operational, we’re looking at a very attractive company that has high chances of being an acquisition target.

data.title: Meta Platforms: Will $600B In AI Spending Pay Off
data.description:
In July I rated Meta Platforms, Inc. (NASDAQ:META)(TSX:META:CA) a Hold, and since then the stock has climbed about 10 percent, topping out at $796 before settling at $771. The move was driven by excitement around artificial intelligence, steady advertising results, and early signs of growth from WhatsApp and Threads.

Meta is valued at around 28 times expected earnings. That is more expensive than Alphabet at 25 times, although Microsoft’s valuation is higher, in the low 30s range. Margins are among the best in the industry, with gross profit close to 82 percent and net profit near 40 percent. Revenue growth is also strong compared to peers. The numbers justify a premium, but the stock does not leave much room for mistakes.

The way I see it, Meta’s $600B+ infrastructure spending, growing regulatory scrutiny, and continued losses in Reality Labs could weigh on free cash flow just as investors have grown comfortable with the story. The market appears to assume Meta will keep its lead in ads and also build entirely new businesses in messaging and hardware. I think that is an optimistic view.

For me, the stock looks fairly priced at these levels. I would hold if already invested, and I would wait for a better entry near $670 if looking to start a position.

What Meta Is Building

Meta is still a giant in online advertising. Its family of apps, which includes Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp, Messenger, and Threads, accounts for almost all of its sales. User activity across these apps remains strong. WhatsApp is beginning to make money from business messaging and payments, and Threads is starting to gain some traction as an alternative to Twitter/X, though it is still in the early stages.

The bigger story is Meta’s push into artificial intelligence. It is building vast computing capacity, hiring top researchers from rivals like Apple, and investing heavily in its own models. This is not a new focus. Meta has quietly relied on AI for a good while, whether in how news feeds are ranked, how ads are targeted, or how harmful content is flagged. Those systems helped Meta keep people engaged and gave advertisers better results, which in turn drove some of the strongest margins in the industry, at around 43 percent. In other words, Meta has already shown it can turn AI research into products that boost financial results.

Looking ahead, the plan to spend more than $600 billion on data centers through 2028 is meant to create the foundation for a new generation of AI tools. The potential upside is large. Businesses worldwide are exploring ways to use generative AI for customer service, sales, and marketing. If Meta can make WhatsApp and Messenger the go-to place for those services, it could tap into a global market worth hundreds of billions of dollars. Adding AI-driven ad features inside Instagram and Facebook could also help it grow faster than the overall digital ad industry, which is still expanding at a healthy pace.

The challenge is that this spending only pays off if Meta can actually sell those services at scale. Companies like NVIDIA make money right away by selling the chips that power AI but Meta has to build its own products on top of its infrastructure. Hardware bets like VR headsets and smart glasses add another layer of risk, since consumer adoption has been slow and Reality Labs continues to post losses.

In my view, Meta’s strengths are clear. It has unmatched reach, long experience in using AI to improve its products, and a very strong balance sheet with nearly $47 billion in cash. That gives it room to take risks. The real test will be how quickly it can turn AI investments into money-making features inside its apps and whether it can break into new areas like business messaging and enterprise tools. If it pulls that off, the returns on its massive spending could be worth it. If not, this era of investment may be seen as overreach.

The Bigger Market Picture

Online advertising is still Meta’s foundation. The industry is growing at a healthy pace, and Meta continues to benefit because it reaches billions of people each month. That scale gives it an edge when it comes to targeting ads.

Regulation, especially in Europe, is becoming a bigger hurdle. New rules like the privacy and safety regulations are already making it harder to use cross-app data, and AI oversight is picking up around the world. Google faces similar issues, but Meta is more exposed because of its heavy reliance on user data.

Meanwhile, progress in AR and VR has been slow, and Amazon is now working on AR glasses. Competition is heating up and Meta’s Reality Labs is still far from proving itself.

Meta remains in a strong position in ads, but rising regulation and tougher competition in hardware could cause problems down the road.

How the Numbers Look

The financial picture remains very solid. Meta’s sales are up about 19 percent over the last year, with forecasts calling for nearly $196 billion in 2025 and close to $228 billion in 2026. Earnings per share are expected at $27.85 next year, rising toward $34.30 by 2027.

Margins remain some of the best in the sector. Gross profit is around 82 percent, operating profit is 43 percent, and net profit is 40 percent. The company holds $47 billion in cash against $49.6 billion in debt, which is manageable given its strong cash flow.

Spending, however, is climbing. Free cash flow growth has slowed to just over 2 percent, showing the impact of rising investments. The dividend, which was recently introduced and yields about 0.28 percent, is modest and signals that most cash will keep going back into the business.

Meta still prints money, but the squeeze on free cash flow is becoming harder to ignore. Investors who expect large buybacks or a higher dividend might be left waiting.

Is the Stock Fairly Priced

At today’s price of $771, Meta trades at roughly 28 times expected earnings. Alphabet trades cheaper at about 24 times, Microsoft is more expensive in the low 30s and NVIDIA is closer to 40 times on forward earnings. The enterprise value to EBITDA multiple is about 20, which is similar to peers.
