The numbers imply that investors expect Meta to keep growing at mid-teens rates and to successfully turn its newer bets into real businesses. Forecasts show revenue rising about 15 percent annually through 2027, which already lines up with that view. Any shortfall would leave the stock exposed.

A discounted cash flow model points to a fair value of about $608 per share, which is below the current market price of $755. This suggests that the stock is trading above intrinsic value and reinforces the view that expectations are already high.

In comparison, Alphabet looks like the safer and cheaper option, while NVIDIA deserves its high multiple because sales are growing more than 70 percent. Meta falls in the middle, profitable and growing but not at a discount.

In my opinion, the current price already reflects the likely outcome. To go higher, Meta must deliver perfect execution. If it stumbles on spending or regulation, the stock has little protection.

What the Headlines Tell Us

The headlines about Meta have been a mix of good and bad. Positive coverage points to the company’s AI hiring spree, the $600B data center investment and new defense contracts for AR goggles. Analysts like Wedbush still call Meta one of the top AI names.

On the negative side, regulators are looking into chatbot safety, reports of teen safety issues are making headlines and regulatory and media scrutiny of chatbot misuse and moderation keeps surfacing. The celebrity chatbot issue involving Taylor Swift and others shows how quickly reputational risk can flare up.

The market seems focused on AI as a driver and is assuming that regulation will stay manageable. My view is that investors are overlooking how much government action could weigh on new revenue sources.

Overall, sentiment is upbeat, but the risks are real and could swing attention fast if optimism fades.

The Main Risks I See

The main risks I see for Meta come from regulators, spending, competition, reputation, and Reality Labs. European privacy rules and U.S. probes into AI chatbots threaten its business model. Spending commitments are huge, and if the payback is slow, shareholders will feel the strain. Competitors are everywhere, with TikTok fighting for time spent, Google competing in ads, and Apple and Amazon entering hardware. Reputation is fragile, and controversies like safety issues and chatbot misuse can quickly erode trust. Reality Labs continues to lose money without showing clear returns.

For me, the largest risk is that Meta spends heavily while failing to turn those investments into steady profits. If that happens, margins and cash flow will shrink.

What Could Drive Growth Ahead

In the short term, Meta’s ad engine remains the most steady growth driver, with 2025 revenue expected to be around $196 billion. Improvements in AI-powered targeting should help sustain double-digit growth, even in a tougher regulatory climate. But the bigger question is whether new platforms can make a real difference to revenue.

WhatsApp is the clearest opportunity. Business messaging is expanding on WhatsApp, and if adoption continues to grow, it could add billions of dollars a year in new revenue on top of Meta’s ad base. The risk is that usage may be strongest in markets where spending per user is lower.

Threads is still early but with Meta’s distribution power, it has the chance to become a mainstream text-based network. Even modest success, say reaching a few hundred million active users, could support ad revenue in the low single-digit billions. However, competition from X and the nonstop wave of new social apps mean Threads has no guarantee of lasting success.

The largest swing factor is AI infrastructure. Meta is spending over $600 billion on data centers through 2028. If it can build revenue-generating services on top of its models, whether through enterprise partnerships, API access, or integrated AI features across its apps, this spending could shift from being just a cost to becoming a real growth driver. If not, investors may view it as a drag on free cash flow.

AR and VR remain uncertain. Meta has secured defense contracts, which create a niche revenue stream, but the bigger consumer bet with Quest headsets and smart glasses has yet to show clear returns. Unless adoption broadens beyond early adopters and gamers, the Reality Labs segment will continue to weigh on results.

The way I see it, Meta has a pipeline of real growth drivers, but each comes with its own set of challenges. WhatsApp monetization looks the most promising in the near term, Threads could add a bit more upside, AI could be transformational if monetized, and AR/VR is a longer-term gamble. The next 12 to 18 months will be about proving whether these bets can deliver more than just promise.

How Things Could Play Out

If things go badly, such as slower ad growth, pressure on profits from spending, or stricter rules on data, the stock could slip back into the $600 range and margins could fall into the mid-30s. If the current course holds, Meta could trade between $725 and $775 with margins around 40 percent and steady revenue from ads and new products. If things go very well, with WhatsApp and Threads turning into meaningful businesses and AI infrastructure producing new services, the stock could rise toward $850 or higher.

In my view, the odds of downside and upside are fairly balanced, which supports keeping the Hold rating.

My Final Take

Meta’s numbers are excellent, but the stock is now priced for a lot of success. Investors are counting on strong growth and on new businesses delivering. That does not leave much room for surprises.

For anyone who already owns the shares, I think holding on makes sense. For anyone thinking of buying, I would wait for a better entry, closer to $670. That price would bring the forward P/E multiple down from about 28 times to closer to 24 times, which is more in line with Alphabet’s valuation. It would also narrow the gap with the DCF fair value estimate of about $608 per share, leaving more margin of safety if Meta’s heavy spending takes longer to pay off. Only clear evidence that WhatsApp, Threads, or AI-driven products are turning into large, profitable businesses would make me want to chase the stock here.

data.title: Cisco: A Solid Tech Name, But Likely To Mirror The Market
data.description:
Cisco Systems, Inc. (NASDAQ:CSCO)(TSX:CSCO:CA) is a technology conglomerate that provides high-quality solutions in networking, cloud management, collaboration, and security. CSCO stock has rallied more than 30% over the past 12 months, given its strong top and bottom line performance and significant demand for its technologies.

The company offers a foundation for a computer network with essential hardware, including access points and routers. CSCO designs security solutions that prevent digital infrastructures from online threats. With AI accelerating, I think it is essential for businesses to have end-to-end security. Cisco is also known for its Webex communication application and cloud solutions, allowing user-friendly management of the company's tools.

CSCO faces significant revenue growth headwinds, but with recent results, it appears that the revenue expansion could rebound. Regardless, the company's top-line grew by 5.30% over the past 12 months, which is below the average company in the benchmark, indicating upside may be limited. The technology conglomerate is highly profitable, but I think it needs to unlock a new revenue growth catalyst to enable multiple expansion.

I think that Cisco Systems is a solid tech name, but it is likely to mirror the market performance. I rate CSCO as a Hold with a $78 price target over the next 18 months. I believe it may be a compelling opportunity for long-term investors who seek a high-quality IT name with an appealing 2.45% dividend yield, but without a new revenue growth catalyst, it may perform in line with the broader market.

FQ4 And FY2025: Double-Beat Story Continues

On August 13, the company reported its FQ4 and FY2025 results. This was the 13th consecutive double-beat quarter for the technology conglomerate. Cisco posted $14.7 billion in revenue, up 8% year-over-year, and exceeded analysts' expectations by $49 million (0.3% surprise). The company posted diluted EPS of $0.99, a roughly 14% increase compared to the same quarter last year, and $0.01 above estimates (1% surprise).

Cisco Systems disclosed FY2025 revenue of $56.7 billion, implying about a 5% increase year-over-year. The company reported $3.81 non-GAAP EPS for the year, signaling 2% bottom-line growth. This has been another strong year for the company, but both top and bottom line growth don't support multiple expansion. CSCO remains a strong, high-quality technology name, but the company's performance doesn't justify market outperformance.

Management has shared that its networking segment, which is the largest business, experienced strong demand and posted $7.63 billion in revenue, up 12% year-over-year. The security segment's revenue increased by 9% over the past 12 months to $1.95 billion in revenue, signaling market-like growth.

On the downside, its second-largest services business was flat year-over-year with total revenue of $3.78 billion. Both collaboration ($1.04 billion in revenue) and observability ($259 million in revenue) categories were weaker, with 2% and 4% revenue growth over the past 12 months, respectively.

Double-beat story continues for Cisco, but the business isn't operating on all cylinders. Half of its business is stagnating, which appears to be concerning for investors, and justifies the stock trading at a lower premium.

On the upside, the company posted significant gross margins of 68.4% (non-GAAP). This supports the narrative of Cisco being highly profitable, and I don't think this outlook will change soon. The technology conglomerate underscored strong AI infrastructure demand with webscale customers' orders exceeding $800 million in FQ4 alone. The company has achieved over $2 billion in AI-related sales for FY2025, which has exceeded its initial target by 100% of $1 billion in revenue for the year.

Cisco Systems continued its shareholder-friendly capital allocation strategy with appealing dividends and a share buyback program. The company has returned $1.6 billion via its quarterly dividends, resulting in $0.41 dividend per share.

CSCO repurchased roughly 19 million shares with an average price per share of $64.65, resulting in $1.25 billion returned value to its shareholders. I think that this should significantly help the bottom-line growth, and the company may see continued upside given its shareholder-friendly capital allocation approach.

I think this was a strong quarter for the company, but a mixed outlook remains. AI-related business experiences substantial demand, while services and collaboration categories struggle to grow market-like pace. This justifies the company trading at a P/E of 17.82 (TTM), a 27% discount versus peers' 24.46x trailing twelve-month multiple, as the market prices in the possibility that CSCO continues growing below the market-average rate.

CSCO Appears To Be Undervalued, But Its Performance Matches The Premium

The company trades at a forward P/E of 16.74, which is a 33% discount to the industry's average of 25.00. CSCO is also trading significantly below the average company of the S&P500 at 23.43x earnings multiple. This is interesting because CSCO is a well-known, premium brand that offers a compelling dividend yield of over 2%, and operates in the technology industry that experiences strong demand given AI-related tailwinds.

As quarterly results indicate, the company is tied to a mixed outlook and single-digit revenue growth. Non-AI-related categories are stagnating or experiencing insignificant growth, which limits the upside and weakens the future outlook for the company. The non-GAAP forward PEG at 2.68 offsets the undervalued narrative for the technology conglomerate, as the company appears to be trading at roughly a 44% premium to the sector median of 1.86.

The forward EV/Sales and Price/Sales at 4.70 and 4.49, respectively, support an overvaluation stance, as the company appears to be trading at approximately 33.5% higher than the industry's average of 3.41 and 3.44. This also signals that the market has partially priced in CSCO's future growth and continued AI expansion. I think, if the company posts strong, above double-digit revenue growth and implies top-line acceleration, CSCO could see significant upside, but until then, the earnings multiple expansion may be limited.

On the upside, the forward EV/EBITDA and EV/EBIT at 12.56 and 13.73, respectively, argue that there is room for multiple expansion, as the company appears to be trading at about a 25% discount at the midpoint compared to peers of 15.32 and 20.58. Valuation metrics confirm the mixed outlook, which could lead to the conclusion that Cisco Systems may actually be matching its premium.

CSCO is a medium-leveraged company with total obligations of $29.64 billion. This is a significant amount that limits the company's financial flexibility, but is also partially offset by $16.67 billion in cash.

The technology company is well-positioned in a high-interest environment. It cost roughly $368 million last quarter for the company to service the debt, but expenses were partially offset by $227 million in interest and investment income.

Yesterday, the Fed lowered interest rates by 0.25%, and I expect the trend will continue. A lower interest-rate environment could help CSCO to refinance its obligations on more favorable conditions, which may result in lower interest expense. This could significantly contribute to bottom-line expansion.

Cisco Systems faces top-line growth headwinds, and that takes the biggest part of the bearish narrative. The forward revenue growth rate of 5.06% implies that continued single-digit top-line growth is likely. This rate also signals 33% underperformance compared to the industry's average of 7.58%.

The company's EBITDA grew by only 0.63% over the past 12 months, which is roughly a 93% difference from the peers' 9.93%. The EBIT has actually declined by nearly 4.3% over the past year, versus the sector median of a 12.1% increase. This justifies CSCO trading at a significant discount to premium and implies that this trend could continue.

CSCO grew its bottom line by 0.39% over the past 12 months, significantly underperforming compared to its peers of 10.40%. The forward diluted EPS growth rate of 5.33% indicates nearly 53% disadvantage compared to the industry's average of 11.27%.

On the upside, Cisco Systems achieved 35% capital expenditures growth on a year-over-year basis, 5x outperformance compared to peers of 7%. The company's operating cash flow grew by 30% over the past 12 months, which also implies 2.4x outperformance versus the sector median of about 13%.

This supports my thesis that, despite CSCO appearing to be undervalued on a forward P/E basis, in terms of expected sales and future growth, it seems to be reasonably priced. I think the market may give a premium for its aggressive capex growth if continued, but the table above indicates that it is within the typical range for the company.

AI-related business could unlock another revenue growth cycle for the company, but until top-line resumes expansion in double digits, I think it is likely for CSCO to follow its bottom-line growth, but not significantly outperform the market in the near-term. The market has a high appetite for AI names, but slightly above double-digit AI-related revenue growth could appear insignificant compared to other peers, such as Arista, grown its revenue by 26% over the past 12 months.

CSCO remains a strong technology name, but it offers business stability and an appealing yield rather than significant revenue growth. It could change, but the company needs to unlock a new top-line growth catalyst.

The company's strength is its profitability. CSCO operates at an above-market gross profit margin (TTM) of about 65%, nearly 31% above the industry average of roughly 49%. The table above indicates that this is a business-typical trait, as CSCO has been operating at above 61% margins since January 2023.

CSCO's EBITDA and EBIT margins (TTM) of 27% and 22%, respectively, imply a significant advantage versus peers of 11% and 6%, more than 3x outperformance. The net income margin (TTM) of roughly 18% is over 4x higher than the sector median of 4.4%. This implies that if the company could resume significant top-line growth, while operating at such profitability metrics, I think that the market would reprice Cisco Systems at a closer to market-average valuation.

Cisco Systems has delivered a significant 10% return on total capital (TTM), approximately 2.7x outperformance compared to the industry's average of 3.77%. The company achieved a substantial 22% return on common equity (TTM), over 4x outperformance versus peers of only 5%.

The technology name earned $14.19 billion in cash from operations (TTM), which was a phenomenal result for the company, indicating a significant advantage compared to the peers' earnings of only $123 million.

Overall, Cisco Systems could appear a compelling long-term investment for investors who seek a stable and robust technology name with a relatively strong moat. Nevertheless, the company offers an appealing dividend yield of 2.39% (TTM), which is a bonus for investors who seek income from their investments.

The 1-year dividend growth rate of 2.53%, 47% below the industry-average of 4.83%, supports the thesis of its strong focus on revenue growth and investments in capital expenditures, but impressive dividend history and the record of 13 years of consecutive payments are intact, and a shareholder-friendly capital allocation approach should continue.

Wall Street forecasts continued 2.45% dividend growth at the midpoint. I totally agree with the estimate. Considering strong AI infrastructure demand, it is likely that CSCO strengthens its position in the high-growth market and seeks double-digit top-line growth.

The market guides for $4.04 EPS for fiscal FY2026. This is actually in line with management's provided guidance with the range of $4.00 to $4.06 EPS. Wall Street forecasts 7.75% bottom-line growth with the $4.36 EPS for fiscal FY2027. I think this is a reasonable estimate, considering CSCO operates at significant margins, applies a shareholder-friendly capital allocation approach with a share repurchasing program, and seeks further top-line expansion.

If we take a $4.36 estimate for fiscal FY2027 and apply an 18x earnings multiple, it comes at roughly my price target of $78 over the next 12 to 18 months. This multiple would be slightly higher than it is right now, given an appealing 2.45% forward dividend yield, continued AI-related revenue expansion, and business stability.

Regardless, the applied forward multiple would be nearly 25% below the average company in the S&P500, and roughly 30% below its IT peers, which would still signal a discount, but would target and justify a reasonable valuation for Cisco Systems, given its single-digit revenue growth and strong investments in CapEx, which could enable AI-expansion in the future.

Main Risks And Concerns

Cisco faces risks and concerns that could significantly affect the stock price in the near term.

Large investments in capital expenditures are capital-intensive and tied to execution risks. Multiple expansion could be driven by the hope of CSCO unlocking a new growth cycle, but if the heavy Capex investments do not result in top-line expansion, the stock could continue trading at undervalued levels compared to the broader market.

Collaboration and services categories appear to be muted with a flat to low single-digit revenue growth. If the economy weakens, those business segments may decline. This could significantly impact sentiment, and the stock could face a sharp selloff.

The broader market has rallied since April lows without a substantial pullback. The same applies to CSCO, with a high double-digit appreciation in the stock price this year. If sentiment changes given uncertainty in the market, stocks could correct, and Cisco would likely be taken down alongside other IT companies.

Cisco Systems operates in a fierce market with other strong names. Highly saturated markets are a concern because there is a possibility of CSCO losing market share to the competition, which could result in missed guidance. This would erode sentiment, and the stock could be repriced.

The bottom-line growth is driven by the shareholder-friendly capital allocation. If management cuts down on buybacks due to underwhelming earnings growth, EPS forecasts could come in short, and the stock may decline.

If none of the above materializes, the company could appreciate in price along with its bottom-line growth. CSCO is a high-quality name that faces revenue growth headwinds, but if management executes well and results exceed expectations, there is plenty of room for multiple expansion.

Conclusion: Cisco Systems Is Fairly Priced, And Needs A Growth Catalyst For Multiple Expansion

I think Cisco Systems could appear compelling for investors who favor a long-term AI-exposure hold with business stability and above-market margins. It also offers an appealing dividend yield, which is appreciated by income-focused investors. The business seeks to resume top-line expansion with heavy investments in Capex, but these are tied to significant execution risks.

The company appears to be trading at an undervalued level compared to both the industry's average and the average company in the S&P500, but fundamental analysis revealed that its partial future growth could already be priced in, and CSCO needs a revenue growth catalyst for a significant multiple expansion.

I believe Cisco Systems is a Hold with a $78 price target over the next 18 months, implying a 15% upside without dividends. I think CSCO should appreciate in price alongside broader market performance, but without a growth catalyst, it is unlikely to outperform the benchmark.

26 Wall Street analysts project a $76 price target over the next 12 months, indicating 12% upside potential. This is in line with my estimate. There are risks tied to CSCO, which could correct the price in the short term, but if none of them materialize and Cisco experiences a strong demand and top-line acceleration, it could reach the price target sooner rather than later.

data.title: U.S. Investment-Grade Company Liquidity Increases In Q2
data.description:
Cash positions for the highest-rated US companies improved in the first quarter of 2025, according to the latest S&P Global Market Intelligence data.

The median cash ratio, which measures cash and equivalents as a percentage of total liabilities, increased quarter over quarter by 1.16 percentage points to 21% for companies rated BBB- or higher by S&P Global Ratings. The metric, calculated quarterly from publicly disclosed corporate balance sheets, reflects a company's ability to pay short-term debt using cash and other liquid assets.

For non-investment-grade companies, the median cash ratio fell to 28% from 28.85% during the first quarter.

Borrowing costs are likely to fall in the near term as the US Federal Reserve is set to push its first rate cut since late 2024. Shorter-dated Treasury yields have fallen in recent days, with the 1-year yield — a reference point for short-term debt interest rates — falling by nearly 50 basis points from its summertime peak.

Median cash ratios (%)

By long-term S&P issuer rating

US companies

Consumer discretionary

Financials

Healthcare

Information technology

Consumer staples

Industrials

Energy

Real estate

Communication services

Utilities

Materials

Data compiled Sept. 5, 2025.

Cash ratio is cash and equivalents as a percentage of total current liabilities. Analysis based on operating US public companies with S&P Global Ratings' long-term credit ratings for securities issued in US dollars.

Companies with S&P Global Ratings' long-term credit ratings of BBB- or higher are considered issuers of investment-grade securities. All other entities with lower ratings are considered issuers of non-investment-grade securities. Ratings may vary from quarter to quarter. In addition, if not rated, a company will be excluded from the analysis.

Financial data based on GAAP filings.

Source: S&P Global Market Intelligence.

© 2025 S&P Global.

Sectors

Median cash ratios improved across seven of 11 market sectors for investment-grade companies, with the sharpest rise recorded for communication services, up 5.13 percentage points to 30.47%.

Ratios fell for investment-grade companies in the financials, information technology, industrials and utilities sectors. IT companies recorded the widest fall in their median cash ratio, dropping 2.49% to 38.31%.

For non-investment-grade companies, four sectors reported an improvement in median cash position: healthcare, real estate, communication services and materials. Real estate companies reported the largest jump with the median cash ratio for non-investment-grade companies jumping 52.05 percentage points to 80.34%.

The largest drop among median cash ratios for lower-rated companies during the quarter was in the IT sector, with a drop of 8.83 percentage points.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Goldman Sachs GQG Partners International Opportunities Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

During the 2Q25 period, the MSCI ACWI increased 11.53% with nine of the 11 sectors exhibiting a positive return. The largest drivers of the benchmark’s gain were Information Technology (+23.4%, contributing 560 bps to the performance of the ACWI index), Financials (+10.7%, +193 bps), and Industrials (+15.5%, +168 bps). From a country perspective, it was a broad-based rally with each of the 20 largest ACWI index constituents rising during the period led by the US (MSCI US +11.5%, contributing 729 bps to the performance of the benchmark), Japan (+11.3%, +56 bps), and Taiwan (+26.4%, +42 bps).

Stylistically, the MSCI ACWI Growth Index (+17.26%) easily outperformed MSCI ACWI Value (+5.84%). Regarding the other primary equity benchmarks, the MSCI ACWI Ex USA, MSCI Emerging Markets, and S&P 500 indices increased 12.03%, 11.99%, and 10.94%, respectively.

In the US, the S&P 500 exhibited significant volatility during the period before ending the quarter at a record high. In early April, the Trump administration announced a 10% baseline incremental tariff on all countries, with higher reciprocal tariff rates on the “worst offenders”, the 60 or so countries with large US trade surpluses. The size of the reciprocal tariffs was more aggressive than expected, including an initial 34% rate on imports from China on top of the 20% tariff increases on China already announced in 1Q25. China subsequently increased its tariffs on imports from the US, resulting in a dramatic tit-for-tat escalation between the two countries. Ten days into April, the US tariffs on imported goods from China had reached ~145% while China’s tariffs on goods imported from the US climbed to 125%.

During the week after the reciprocal tariff announcement, the US equity market sold off 11%, which represented a 19% drawdown from the S&P 500’s prior all-time high established in mid-February 2025, while the yield on the 10-year US Treasury spiked by almost 50 bps. The large and swift decline in the prices of equities and bonds in the wake of the new trade policies prompted the Trump administration to pause the reciprocal tariffs for 90 days on all countries except for China. Various overlapping automotive tariffs were also relaxed as the month progressed. Near the end of April, the Trump administration signaled they were willing to negotiate with major trade partners, including China, and the US equity market nearly recovered the entirety of its intra-month drawdown.

In early May, investor sentiment improved after the US and China unexpectedly announced an easing of tariffs between the two countries. Washington and Beijing agreed to keep the new lower tariff levels in place for 90 days, with the goal of working toward a broader trade deal in the interim. The Trump administration also extended into July a tariff deadline on the EU after the president of the European Commission indicated the region was ready to advance talks swiftly and decisively. President Trump also made a lucrative mid-month trip to the Middle East that ended with investment agreements with Saudi Arabia, Qatar, and the United Arab Emirates worth an estimated $2 trillion for projects in defense, aviation, and artificial intelligence. Equities in the US continued their rally in June as benign inflation data fueled investor optimism that the central bank will reduce interest rates in the second half of 2025.

The US 10-year Treasury yield entered the quarter at 4.21%, peaked in mid-May at 4.61%, and ended the period at 4.23%. We believe the 10-year yield fell during the last six weeks of the quarter as the market was pricing in the likely loosening of Fed monetary policy. Investment grade credit spreads in the US narrowed 11 bps in 2Q25 to 86 bps, while high yield spreads tightened by 59 bps to 296 bps. The Bloomberg US Aggregate bond index (largely US Treasuries, investment grade corporates, and agency mortgage-backed securities) had a small gain of 0.3% during the quarter. The US Dollar Index (DXY) weakened 7.0% in the period on mounting concerns about the size of the federal debt and the political pressure on the central bank to reduce the overnight policy rate.

The Bloomberg Commodity Index declined 4.1% during 2Q25 as losses in energy (natural gas -20.7%, Brent crude oil -11.8%), and iron ore (-7.8%) were only partially offset by a gain in gold (+5.7%). We believe natural gas declined due to plant outages and maintenance at the country’s largest liquefied natural gas export facility in Texas, resulting in the availability of higher domestic supply. Regarding oil, we believe the weaker prices were driven by OPEC’s announcement of an increase in production starting in May and concerns about the stability of oil demand while the US continues to negotiate new tariffs with its trade partners. Of note, oil futures increased as much as 26% in June after Israel and the US bombed targets related to Iran’s nuclear program, with investors concerned that Tehran might retaliate by choking off the Strait of Hormuz, a key bottleneck for Middle East oil exports. However, a cease fire was negotiated quickly with most of the oil price spike dissipating by the end of the month.

Drivers of Return:

During 2Q25, the GS GQG Partners International Opportunities Fund (MUTF:GSIHX) underperformed the MSCI ACWI ex-USA (Net) benchmark by 499 basis points net of fees. The portfolio had a net return of 7.04% versus the benchmark’s 12.03%.

From a sector perspective, Q2 relative performance was negatively impacted by an overweight to and selection within Energy. An underweight to Information Technology further detracted from relative performance. Relative underperformance was partially offset by an underweight to Consumer Discretionary and an underweight to Materials.

From a regional perspective, the Fund’s stock selection in Europe (ex-UK) was the largest detractor.

Key relative contributors:

Rolls-Royce Holdings plc - Rolls-Royce Holdings plc develops and services power and propulsion systems for aircraft, boats, submarines, and electricity generation. The company is headquartered in the United Kingdom. In May, despite increased uncertainty caused by global tariffs, Rolls-Royce re-affirmed its financial guidance due to solid revenue from its civil aerospace unit’s aftermarket services business, higher orders in its power-systems division, and robust defense demand.

Alibaba Group Holding Limited - Alibaba is an e-commerce company based in China. It operates a market for customer shopping, a third-party marketing platform for brands as well as retailers, and a cloud computing business. Alibaba declined during the period as falling growth and compressed margins in its cloud business led to revenue and net income prints that were below consensus expectations. This was a positive contributor to relative performance this quarter as we did not own the stock.

Philip Morris International Inc. - Philip Morris International manufactures cigarettes, smoke-free products, associated electronic devices, and other nicotine-containing products in markets outside the United States. The company is based in New York. For 1Q25, PM delivered sales and EPS growth that were ahead of sell-side consensus expectations by 3% and 5%, respectively, as the company exhibited volume growth in combustibles and in smoke free products. Shipments of its Zyn nicotine pouches increased more than 50% as production capacity increased ahead of schedule during the quarter.

Tencent Holdings Limited - Tencent offers online games as well as financial technology, cloud computing, and social network services. The company is based in China. During our holding period, the shares declined as the tariff rhetoric between the US and China escalated dramatically with reciprocal tariffs exceeding 100%. This was a contributor on a relative basis because we were underweight the stock during the quarter.

British American Tobacco p.l.c.- British American Tobacco is a London based manufacturer of tobacco and nicotine products to consumers worldwide. Their primary brands include Newport and Camel in the U.S. as well as Dunhill, Kent, Pall Mall, Lucky Strike, and Rothmans. BAT indicated that its revenue in the first half was slightly better than expected, and raised its outlook for the year due to higher cigarette volumes and strong demand for its nicotine pouches.

Key relative detractors:

Petróleo Brasileiro S.A. – Petrobras - Petrobras is an oil and gas exploration and production company with additional operations in refining, transportation, petrochemicals, and power generation. The firm is headquartered in Brazil. Petrobras sold off as crude oil prices declined during the quarter due to OPEC’s announcement of an increase in production and concerns about the stability of oil demand while the US negotiated new tariffs with its trade partners.

Chubb Limited- Chubb operates as a property and casualty insurance company. The company provides commercial and personal property, casualty, and personal accident and supplemental health insurance, reinsurance, and life insurance to a diverse group of global clients. Chubb is headquartered in Switzerland. Despite reporting better than expected 1Q25 EPS driven by lower than expected catastrophe losses, the company missed consensus estimates on premium growth, causing the stock to decline.

AstraZeneca PLC- AstraZeneca is a UK based biopharmaceutical company focusing on the discovery, development, and commercialization of prescription medicines for oncology, cardiovascular, renal, metabolism, and respiratory patients. The shares sold off in early April after the Trump administration announced a 10% baseline tariff on all countries. AZN generates 40% of its revenue in the US.

TotalEnergies SE- Total is an oil and gas producer with additional operations in liquefied natural gas (LNG), renewable energy sources, refining, and petrochemicals. The company is headquartered in France. Total’s shares sold off as crude oil prices declined during the quarter due to OPEC’s announcement of an increase in production and concerns about the stability of oil demand while the US negotiated new tariffs with its trade partners.

Nestlé S.A.- Nestle is a food and beverage company with more than 2,000 brands marketed around the world, including Nescafe coffee, KitKat candy, and Purina pet food. The company is headquartered in Switzerland. We think there was potential investor concern about cuts to the US food stamp program which could negatively impact volumes for certain packaged-food companies. The US makes up approximately 30% of Nestle’s revenue.

Positioning and Potential Headwinds:

The Consumer Staples and Utilities sectors are the largest overweights in the portfolio with the Consumer Discretionary and Information Technology sectors the largest underweights. Potential headwinds include regulatory risks for names in the Utilities sector including rate determinations and approvals of cap ex plans. Another potential headwind is a continued increase in AI-related spending that could drive positive EPS revisions in the tech sector, which would likely impact our relative performance. We expect to monitor the relevant data closely and adapt to the changing environment.

On a country basis, India, the United Kingdom, and the United States are the largest overweights while Japan and China are the largest underweights. potential headwinds include a weather shock in India that could adversely impact agricultural output, raise food prices, and impact consumer spending in other areas.

Outlook

We are overweight the Consumer Staples and Utilities sectors. In Consumer Staples, we believe the fundamentals for certain tobacco businesses are solid with growth at some companies coming from next generation products. In addition, select tobacco stocks have what we consider to be attractive dividend yields. Regarding Utilities, we believe the European regulatory environment for power utilities appears to be improving as companies seek an acceptable rate of return on the investment required to grow the supply of electricity. Meanwhile, we have identified several utilities focused on power transmission where we believe there is high visibility on revenue and earnings.

We are also overweight the Energy sector, focusing on certain companies with diversified operations, including upstream exploration and development businesses as well as downstream refining capability. The price of oil may be negatively impacted in the near term by concern about a tariff-induced economic slowdown and OPEC adding to supply to push back on shale production, but we think this is priced into the stocks we own. We believe the supply-demand dynamic will eventually balance while the capital return-oriented investment theses for these companies remain intact. We also like certain midstream companies, as we view these pipeline operators as utility-like businesses that may generate high single-digit earnings growth while their stocks offer what we consider to be attractive dividend yields.

The two largest underweights from a sector perspective are in Consumer Discretionary and Information Technology. We are skeptical that the current run rate of AI-related cap ex spending is sustainable and believe certain tech names are overearning. In addition, many stocks in the sector are expensive, in our opinion, with a risk of compression in their valuation multiples.

Potential headwinds include regulatory risks for names in the Utilities sector including rate determinations and approvals of cap ex plans. Another potential headwind is the recent increase in tariffs by the Trump administration. If there is a tariff-induced slowdown in economic activity, that may result in lower demand for crude oil, which could negatively impact earnings growth in the Energy sector. Another potential headwind is a continued increase in AI-related spending that could drive positive EPS revisions in the tech sector, which would likely impact our relative performance. Finally, a weather shock in India could adversely impact agricultural output, raise food prices, and impact consumer spending in other areas. We expect to monitor the relevant data closely and adapt to the changing environment.

While we are not economists, we do monitor macro data as part of our risk framework. We underwrite the existing, and potential, companies in our portfolios focusing on the visibility of their future free cash flows. Ignoring the macro conditions in which these companies operate would impact the accuracy of any investor’s earnings outlook, in our opinion. However, adapting our portfolios to reflect new information is a hallmark of our process and we will continue to go where the data leads us.

Information Benchmarks

The MSCI USA Growth Index captures large and mid cap securities exhibiting overall growth style characteristics in the US. The growth investment style characteristics for index construction are defined using five variables: long-term forward EPS growth rate, short-term forward EPS growth rate, current Internal growth rate and long- term historical EPS growth trend and long-term historical sales per share growth trend.

The MSCI USA Index is designed to measure the performance of the large and mid cap segments of the US market. With 609 constituents, the index covers approximately 85%of the free float adjusted market capitalization in the US.

The MSCI China Index captures large and mid cap representation across China A shares, H shares, B shares, Red chips, P chips and foreign listings (e.g. ADRs). With 717 constituents, the index covers about 85% of this China equity universe. Currently, the index includes Large Cap A and Mid Cap A shares represented at 20% of their free float adjusted market capitalization.

The MSCI All Country World Index (MSCI ACWI) captures large and mid cap representation across 23 Developed Markets (OM) and 24 Emerging Markets (EM) countries. With 2,921 constituents, the index covers approximately 85% of the global investable equity opportunity set. DM countries Include: Australia, Austria, Belgium, Canada, Denmark, Finland, France, Germany, Hong Kong, Ireland, Israel, Italy, Japan, Netherlands, New Zealand, Norway, Portugal, Singapore, Spain, Sweden, Switzerland, the UK and the US. EM countries include: Brazil, Chile, China, Colombia, Czech Republic, Egypt, Greece, Hungary, India, Indonesia, Korea, Kuwait, Malaysia, Mexico, Peru, Philippines, Poland, Qatar, Saudi Arabia, South Africa, Taiwan, Thailand, Turkey and United Arab Emirates.

The MSCI All Country World ex USA Index (MSCI ACWI ex USA) captures large and mid cap representation across 22 of 23 Developed Markets (DM) countries (excluding the US) and 24 Emerging Markets (EM) countries. With 2,312 constituents, the index covers approximately 85% of the global equity opportunity set outside the US. OM countries include: Australia, Austria, Belgium, Canada, Denmark, Finland, France, Germany, Hong Kong, Ireland, Israel, Italy, Japan, Netherlands, New Zealand, Norway, Portugal, Singapore, Spain, Sweden, Switzerland and the UK. EM countries include: Brazil, Chile, China, Colombia, Czech Republic, Egypt, Greece, Hungary, India, Indonesia, Korea, Kuwait, Malaysia, Mexico, Peru, Philippines, Poland, Qatar, Saudi Arabia, South Africa, Taiwan, Thailand, Turkey and United Arab Emirates.

The MSCI Emerging Markets Index captures large and mid cap representation across 24 Emerging Markets (EM) countries. With 1,441constituents, the index covers approximately 85% of the free float-adjusted market capitalization in each country. EM countries include: Brazil, Chile, China, Colombia, Czech Republic, Egypt, Greece, Hungary, India, Indonesia, Korea, Kuwait, Malaysia, Mexico, Peru, Philippines, Poland, Qatar, Saudi Arabia, South Africa, Taiwan, Thailand, Turkey and United Arab Emirates.

The S&P 500® Index is a widely used stock market index that can serve as barometer of US stock market performance, particularly with respect to larger capitalization stocks. It is a market weighted index of stocks of 500 leading companies in leading industries and represents a significant portion of the market value of all stocks publicly traded in the United States.
